name: 221B Baker Street
certaintyNote: The case opens at Holmes and Watson's rooms at 221B Baker Street in London, the fictional address on the real street; sited there, outside the Dartmoor setting.
note: At Baker Street, Dr Mortimer lays before Holmes the legend of the Baskerville hound and the strange death of Sir Charles, and Holmes reads a whole history from a forgotten walking-stick. Doyle begins in the familiar London rooms before dispatching Watson to the moor, keeping the great detective offstage to sharpen the suspense of the wild country to come.

sources.ref: The Hound of the Baskervilles, Wikipedia, on the opening

name: Baskerville Hall
certaintyNote: Baskerville Hall, the cursed family seat on Dartmoor, is a fictional house drawn from the great houses of the Devon moor; sited there.
note: Watson escorts the new heir, the sturdy Sir Henry Baskerville, to the grim old hall set amid the Dartmoor wastes, where an escaped convict roams and every neighbour hides something. Doyle steeps the house in dread, the baying heard across the moor at night and the ancient curse pressing on a man who refuses to be frightened from his own home.

sources.ref: The Hound of the Baskervilles, Wikipedia, on Sir Henry and the Hall

name: The Grimpen Mire
certaintyNote: The deadly Grimpen Mire, a bog that swallows ponies and men, is drawn from the real treacherous mires of Dartmoor such as Fox Tor Mire; sited on the moor.
note: Out on the moor lies the Great Grimpen Mire, a green, sucking bog that only a hidden path crosses, and into which the unwary vanish without trace. Doyle makes the mire the story's most sinister feature, a natural trap that conceals both the villain's secret lair and, in the end, the means of his own destruction.

sources.ref: The Hound of the Baskervilles, Wikipedia, on the Grimpen Mire

name: Merripit House
certaintyNote: Merripit House, the moorland home of the butterfly-collecting naturalist Stapleton, is drawn from the isolated dwellings of Dartmoor; sited on the moor.
note: At lonely Merripit House lives the mild naturalist Stapleton, who chases butterflies across the moor and hides, behind his affable manner, both a false marriage and a murderous design on the Baskerville fortune. Doyle plants his villain in plain sight, the outwardly harmless collector who has bred and starved a real hound to play the family ghost.

sources.ref: The Hound of the Baskervilles, Wikipedia, on Stapleton

name: The hound on the moor
certaintyNote: The climax, in which the phosphor-painted hound is loosed through the fog and Holmes reveals the human hand behind the legend, is set on the open moor; sited in the setting.
note: In the fog a monstrous hound, its muzzle daubed with phosphorus to glow like a fiend, comes bounding after Sir Henry, and Holmes, waiting unseen, shoots it dead and unmasks Stapleton, who flees to his death in the mire. Doyle ends by dissolving the supernatural terror into a coldly rational crime, the ghostly hound revealed as paint, hunger and human greed.

sources.ref: The Hound of the Baskervilles, Wikipedia, on the climax
